retrieved_docs.content: Sufficient and stable funding for HIV programming is of critical importance, influencing the availability and penetration of interventions, particularly in low- and middle-income countries. [24] By 2019, Eswatini, Namibia, and Rwanda exceeded the UNAIDS target of 73% viral suppression across all HIV-positive people.

retrieved_docs.content: Currently, there is no vaccine or cure for HIV, though clinical trials and studies continue to be performed. ART, when started early, has enabled people with HIV to live long lives with chronic infection. [2] The CDC aims to implement a 90-90-90 plan, where 90% of people infected with HIV have been diagnosed, 90% with diagnosed infection are on ART, and 90% of those on ART have decreased viral load. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Abacavir -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify patients who require antiretroviral therapy, including abacavir, for managing HIV-1 based on clinical guidelines and diagnostic criteria. Implement and integrate abacavir into antiretroviral regimens efficiently, adhering to prescribed dosages and appropriate combinations as per established HIV treatment protocols. Assess patient responses to abacavir therapy regularly, monitoring treatment adherence, viral load suppression, and potential drug-related complications through laboratory tests and clinical evaluations. Determine strategies with other healthcare team members to ensure seamless implementation, monitoring, and optimization of abacavir therapy within the broader context of HIV management. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Highly Active Antiretroviral Therapy (HAART) -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Reduce morbidity and mortality (AIDS and non-AIDS associated causes) Improve the quality of life Reduce plasma viral RNA load Prevent transmission to others (sex partners, needle-sharing partners, mother to infant) Prevent drug resistance Improve immune function

The World Health Organization (WHO) advises one month of ART with at least three medications as post-exposure prophylaxis. [24]

For example, recommendations for PrEP have not been adopted in national guidelines in Russia, China, Angola, Libya, Sudan, and Venezuela, amongst others . [WHO. HIV Policy Adoption Fact Sheet. 2023] The addition of PrEP to locally available prevention measures is necessary to end global transmission of HIV, particularly in areas with a high burden of HIV. [37]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV and AIDS -- Treatment / Management -- Inadequate Viral Suppression and Development of Resistance
retrieved_docs.content: When inadequate viral suppression occurs, the HIV viral load and selection pressure increase, inducing mutations favorable to resistance against active antiretroviral treatments. Virological failure occurs when HIV viral is above 1000 copies/mL on 2 separate and consecutive measurements over 3 months while on current antiretroviral treatment for 6 months or longer. This condition can result in clinical failure, described as a new event or recurrence of WHO stage 4 severe immunodeficiency after 6 months of current effective antiretroviral treatment. Clinical failure suggests concern for antiretroviral resistance while a patient adheres to effective antiretroviral treatment for 6 months or longer. [63] Given HIV has an RNA genome prone to mutations, selection pressure from active antiretroviral agents during reverse transcriptase can encourage drug-resistant mutants that lead to virological failure, clinical failure, and eventually immunological failure, defined as a CD4+ count of 250 cells/mm 3 or less. [64] [65] Thus, while trials such as NADIA and REVAMP indicated treatment regimens incorporating antiretroviral agents with prior resistance is feasible, the theoretical basis of how HIV develops new mutations with inadequate viral suppression should be considered by the treating clinician in managing treatment-experienced patients. [33] [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Funding
retrieved_docs.content: Sufficient and stable funding for HIV programming is of critical importance for preventing HIV, influencing the availability and penetration of interventions, particularly in low- and middle-income countries. [98] Yet total funding for HIV programming in low- and middle-income countries was 2.6% lower in 2022 than in 2021, the third annual decrease in a row and well short of what is needed to achieve targets.[UNAIDS. Global Report. 2023] External funding has decreased for several years, with low- and middle-income countries funding about 60% of their HIV programming in 2022, up from about 50% in 2010.[UNAIDS. Global Report. 2023] This gap in funding has increased since the COVID-19 pandemic emerged.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Prevention -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Improving Access to Quality HIV Prevention and Care Services
retrieved_docs.content: Including communities and civil society in advocacy, service delivery, and policy-making ensures that services are culturally appropriate and responsive to the needs of the population it serves. For example, partnering with community organizations has been shown to improve linkage to care for HIV treatment, decreasing the time to viral suppression and reducing the risk of HIV transmission. [38]  When implemented broadly in locally defined at-risk populations, the use of PrEP as prescribed has a powerful influence on reducing incident HIV transmission at the population level. Community-level interventions, such as door-to-door HIV prevention services, are beneficial in areas of high prevalence, such as urban areas with high levels of poverty.[CDC. HIV - Communities in Crisis. 2019]

retrieved_docs.content: A clear understanding of the social context in which HIV infection occurs is vital to providing high-quality programming. [23] For example, while TasP use has increased over time, particularly among MSM, its optimal use is hampered by disbelief that U=U among people who do not have HIV and those living in Africa. Acceptability is high once the science is understood. [22] HIV care and prevention models that address the overlapping clinical and social complexities faced by many equity-deserving populations can lead to better clinical outcomes. Structural interventions to improve socioeconomic conditions may also reduce HIV infection rates.[CDC. HIV - Communities in Crisis. 2019]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV and AIDS -- Treatment / Management -- Monitoring Following Initiation of Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: When treatment is initiated, the patient's HIV viral load should be evaluated in 2 to 4 weeks and no later than 8 weeks. The viral load can be rechecked every 4 to 8 weeks to ensure the levels decline. Virologic suppression to undetectable levels (defined as an HIV RNA level of <200 copies/mL) may take up to 24 weeks of continuous therapy. If the HIV RNA level has not declined by 2 log10 copies/mL within 12 weeks and adherence is confirmed, evaluation for resistance with genotype testing for the patient's regimen is recommended. Genotype resistance testing should also be obtained if virologic suppression is not achieved. [13] When viral suppression is established, the viral load should be monitored every 3 to 4 months. [7] An HIV RNA level that remains persistently below this lower limit of detection demonstrates sustained virologic suppression—the primary goal of HIV therapy. Please see StatPearls' companion resource, " HIV Antiretroviral Therapy, " for more information.

retrieved_docs.title: Abacavir -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Recommendations for Increasing Successful Outcomes in Antiretroviral Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Initiation of highly active antiretroviral therapy (HAART) with a high degree of adherence, defined as greater than 95% refills and plasma drug concentrations above the therapeutic steady-state concentration, is associated with low levels of antiretroviral resistance. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 706
retrieved_docs.content: HIV is a chronic viral infection that can be managed with antiretroviral therapy, and tenofovir disoproxil is an antiretroviral prodrug that may be used to treat HIV infection. Tenofovir disoproxil works by inhibiting viral replication, thereby helping to suppress HIV viral load, preserve immune function, and reduce disease progression, making it a key component of comprehensive HIV patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 135
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is strongly associated with shared needle use, which constitutes a major transmission route, and its prevention and treatment rely on antiretroviral agents classified by mechanism of action. Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) target the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme, mimic natural nucleosides, and cause chain termination, thereby inhibiting viral DNA synthesis. Key NRTIs include tenofovir disoproxil, abacavir, didanosine (dideoxycytidine), emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, and zidovudine; each has demonstrated activity in pre‑exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) and in early or combination treatment regimens to suppress viral replication. The non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor delavirdine mesylate also possesses preventive properties by directly inhibiting reverse transcriptase. Integrase inhibitors—cabotegravir, dolutegravir, elvitegravir, and raltegravir—block the viral integrase enzyme, preventing integration of viral DNA into the host genome; cabotegravir is notable for its dual use in both treatment of established infection and PrEP, while the other agents are primarily employed for treatment but may be used preventively in high‑risk settings. Collectively, the correct classification and combined use of NRTIs, non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors, and integrase inhibitors form the backbone of modern HIV prevention strategies and therapeutic protocols, optimizing patient outcomes through viral suppression and reduced acquisition risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 1216
retrieved_docs.content: Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors constitute a class of antiretroviral agents that target the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme, thereby blocking viral replication. Tenofovir disoproxil, abacavir, dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, and stavudine are all members of this nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitor class. By incorporating these drugs into combination antiretroviral therapy, clinicians can achieve more effective suppression of HIV, improve patient outcomes, and reduce the risk of resistance development.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2490
retrieved_docs.content: Amprenavir is an antiretroviral medication that functions as an inhibitory proteas, meaning it acts as a protease inhibitor that blocks the activity of viral proteases essential for HIV replication, highlighting its therapeutic role in managing HIV infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 276
retrieved_docs.content: Cabotegravir, dolutegravir, and raltegravir are all members of the pharmacologic class known as integrase inhibitors, which are systemic anti‑infective agents used primarily in the treatment of HIV infection. As integrase inhibitors, each drug targets the HIV integrase enzyme, preventing the integration of viral DNA into the host genome and thereby suppressing viral replication. Their inclusion in this class highlights a shared mechanism of action and therapeutic role in antiretroviral regimens, making them important options for clinicians managing patients with HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 776
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirals for treatment of HIV infections, combinations are classified as direct acting antivirals, meaning they target specific steps of the HIV replication cycle. Within this category, the fixed‑dose combination of abacavir / lamivudine is listed as a member, representing one of the therapeutic regimens used to treat HIV infection by providing synergistic antiviral activity and helping to prevent the development of drug resistance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 90
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is strongly associated with shared‑needle use, which constitutes a major route of viral transmission. A broad spectrum of antiretroviral agents has been identified for both prevention (pre‑exposure prophylaxis) and treatment of HIV infection. Preventive activity has been reported for delavirdin, delavirdin‑mesylate, dolutegravir, efavirenz, elvitegravir, emtricitabine, etravirine, lamivudine, nevirapine, raltegravir, rilpivirine, stavudine, zidovudine and tenofovir disoproxil, whereas cabotegravir is highlighted for its therapeutic effect in established infection. These drugs belong to three principal pharmacologic classes. Non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) – delavirdin, rilpivirine, efavirenz, etravirine and nevirapine – bind directly to the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme and inhibit viral replication. Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) – dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, zidovudine and tenofovir disoproxil – require intracellular phosphorylation and act as chain terminators of reverse transcription. Integrase inhibitors – cabotegravir, dolutegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir – target the HIV integrase enzyme, blocking integration of viral DNA into the host genome. Understanding the transmission risk posed by shared‑needle use together with the preventive and therapeutic properties of these NNRTIs, NRTIs and integrase inhibitors, and their class‑specific mechanisms, informs comprehensive strategies for HIV prevention, combination antiretroviral therapy, resistance mitigation and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2156
retrieved_docs.content: Dolutegravir, elvitegravir, and raltegravir are all members of the class of integrase inhibitors that are used as systemic anti‑infective agents. As integrase inhibitors, these drugs target the HIV integrase enzyme, preventing the integration of viral DNA into the host genome and thereby forming a key component of antiretroviral therapy for HIV infection. Recognizing that each of these agents belongs to the same pharmacologic class helps clinicians select appropriate regimens, understand class‑related efficacy and safety profiles, and manage drug interactions in the care of patients living with HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 1025
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is closely associated with the practice of shared needle use, which represents a major route of transmission. In patients with HIV infection, the use of methotrexate sodium is contraindicated because of potential adverse interactions. Several antiretroviral agents have preventive activity against HIV infection, including delavirdine‑mesylate, dolutegravir, elvitegravir, and raltegravir. In addition to prevention, cabotegravir and fostemsavir are employed as therapeutic agents for the treatment of established HIV infection. Dolutegravir, elvitegravir, cabotegravir, and raltegravir are all members of the Integrase Inhibitors class of systemic anti‑infectives, reflecting their shared mechanism of targeting the viral integrase enzyme. This network of transmission risk, drug contraindications, preventive agents, and treatment options underscores the importance of integrated management strategies for individuals at risk for or living with HIV infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 113
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is closely linked to the behavioral risk factor of shared needle use, underscoring the importance of harm‑reduction counseling in prevention strategies. A broad spectrum of antiretroviral agents demonstrates preventive activity against HIV acquisition, including delavirdin, delavirdin‑mesylate, dolutegravir, efavirenz, elvitegravir, emtricitabine, etravirine, lamivudine, nevirapine, raltegravir, rilpivirine, stavudine, zidovudine and tenofovir disoproxil. Cabotegravir is recognized for therapeutic use in established HIV infection. These drugs are organized into pharmacologic classes that reflect distinct mechanisms of action: non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) comprise doravirine, delavirdin, efavirenz, etravirine, rilpivirine and nevirapine, which bind to a specific site on HIV reverse transcriptase; nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) include dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, zidovudine and tenofovir disoproxil, acting as intracellularly phosphorylated chain terminators; and integrase inhibitors—cabotegravir, dolutegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir—target viral DNA integration. Notable drug‑drug contraindications are identified within these classes: doravirine is contraindicated with rifapentine, and rilpivirine is contraindicated with pantoprazole, highlighting the need for careful medication reconciliation. Recognizing the relationships among needle‑sharing risk, the preventive and therapeutic roles of specific antiretrovirals, their class‑based mechanisms, and relevant contraindications is essential for constructing safe, effective HIV prophylaxis and treatment regimens and for optimizing patient counseling and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1227
retrieved_docs.content: Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors constitute a class of antiretroviral drugs that target the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme, thereby blocking viral replication. This therapeutic class includes tenofovir disoproxil, emtricitabine, lamivudine, zidovudine, and dideoxycytidine (also known as zalcitabine). By sharing a common mechanism of action, these agents are frequently combined in HIV treatment regimens to achieve synergistic viral suppression and reduce the risk of resistance development.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2032
retrieved_docs.content: Cabotegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir are all members of the pharmacologic class identified as Integrase Inhibitors, antiinfectives for systematic use. As integrase inhibitors, each of these agents targets the HIV integrase enzyme, thereby preventing the integration of viral DNA into the host genome and serving as essential components of systemic antiretroviral therapy. Their shared classification underscores a common mechanism of action and therapeutic purpose in the treatment of HIV infection, highlighting the importance of this drug class in modern HIV management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2656
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors, classified as direct‑acting antivirals, comprise a group of medications that target the HIV protease enzyme to prevent viral replication. Key members of this protease inhibitor class include ritonavir, fosamprenavir, amprenavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir, each sharing the common mechanism of protease inhibition while differing in pharmacokinetic properties and clinical use. Recognizing these agents as part of the broader protease inhibitor, direct‑acting antiviral category is essential for selecting appropriate antiretroviral regimens and optimizing patient care in HIV infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1226
retrieved_docs.content: Integrase Inhibitors, antiinfectives for systematic use constitute a class of antiretroviral agents that act by inhibiting the HIV integrase enzyme, thereby preventing viral DNA from integrating into the host genome. Cabotegravir, dolutegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir are all members of this class and are employed systemically in the treatment of HIV infection. By targeting the same viral integration step, these drugs provide essential options for combination antiretroviral therapy, contributing to effective viral suppression and improved patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 0 -- Community 3
retrieved_docs.content: preventive and therapeutic use enables precise prescribing, optimized patient care, and effective public‑health interventions across viral diseases such as HIV, ocular viral infections, influenza, smallpox, and related complications.
